text: I am sharing a case of a perforated appendicitis. This was a patient who had actually presented to an emergency room about four days earlier with some abdominal pains, subjective fever and chills. They had gotten a non-contrasted CT scan, which was not concerning for appendicitis, and she was discharged with antibiotics for a presumed urinary tract infection. Four days later, she came back. She had very high fevers, a hundred and three, more severe abdominal pain that was really bad in the right groin, was tachycardic. And by the time I was called to see her in the emergency room, I decided to take her straight to the operating room. I did not re-image her. So here we are.

The laparoscopic view from the umbilical port shows the gangrenous appendix walled off between the terminal ileum and retroperitoneum, with lateral attachment of the terminal ileum being divided. The surgeon navigates around the right iliacs and ureter, repositioning the small bowel out of the pelvis. The view reveals congealed small bowel loops with purulent peritonitis, stuck to an abscess cavity. The surgeon continues dissection to fully expose the appendix, ensuring critical structures are not compromised during division.

The surgeon unroofs the abscess cavity and continues to bluntly lift the small bowel away from the abscess into the upper abdomen. The gangrenous appendix is visible in the center of the screen, with its tip clearly identifiable. The surgeon assesses the surrounding area, confirming the absence of critical structures like the ureter, and prepares to divide a band of tissue.

The surgeon lifts the small bowel, revealing the appendix, which is now completely free. The surgeon assesses the base of the appendix, considering options for closure, and decides to dissect through the mesentery back to the base. The tip of the appendix is lifted from the right pelvic side wall, explaining the patient's right groin pain.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the appendix, avoiding avulsion as it is freed from the surrounding tissue. The necrotic back wall of the appendix is visible, and the surgeon cautiously progresses towards the base, ensuring the ileocecal valve is not in the operative field. A small nub is identified, and the surgeon assesses the base of the appendix for further action.

The surgeon identifies a one-centimeter stump of the appendix and uses a stapler to secure closure of the inflamed tissue. The appendix is then removed from the abdomen, followed by irrigation of the surgical area.

The surgeon irrigates the surgical field with approximately two liters of saline, ensuring thorough removal of loculated abscesses between the loops of the bowel. The irrigation process is carefully conducted to clear any remaining collections in the area.

The surgeon assesses the retroperitoneum, noting it appears clean except for a fibrotic plaque. A drain is placed in the surgical site to mitigate the risk of a delayed abscess. Omentum is positioned in the pelvis as part of the closure process.
